concept: Forget / Lose from Memory / Let Slip Away
description: To forget: names, appointments, grievances. The unwilled loss of what memory held — and sometimes the willed release of it. Companion and opposite of 'tole'-descended remembering words and of 'hekawi' (find); what is forgotten can be found again.
sound_symbolism: Soft 's', open 'a', then 'hu' — the breath that carries the memory away. Nothing sharp: forgetting in Phi sounds like what it mostly is, a gentle leak rather than a theft.
grammatical_notes: Transitive: 'mia nomei to sahu.' (I forgot the name.) Negated for reassurance: 'mia thia ma sahu.' (I do not forget you.) With the inchoative: 'mia pa sahu.' (I am beginning to forget.)

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Impermanence includes memory. Naming forgetting without shame lets speakers hold their own minds gently — and distinguishes the forgetting that wounds from the forgetting that frees.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Communities that can say 'I forgot' plainly need fewer excuses; the honest verb is the shorter path back to trust.

tags.temporal: What time does to the unrehearsed
